narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 67.4% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record in law school, particularly your placement in the second decile for grades in your first and third years, which stood out as the top two factors pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Students who typically pass the bar average around the sixth decile in those years, so your position lower in the rankings signaled potential challenges in sustaining the performance needed for the exam. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.70 is a bright spot—actually stronger than the 3.24 average among those who pass and the third most influential factor—it wasn't enough to offset those earlier concerns, as it pulled the prediction slightly in the direction of success. Your LSAT score of 31, below the 37.27 average for passers, also played a role in the overall risk assessment, though less dominantly than your decile rankings. Coming from a middle-income family aligns with over a third of successful bar takers, and opting for full-time study matches the vast majority who pass, but these didn't carry enough weight to shift the outcome. In essence, the model saw your profile as riskier than the typical accepted applicant, whose stronger year-one and year-three deciles correlate more reliably with bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, as that could recalibrate future predictions favorably. Strengthening your law school grades to climb into higher deciles would directly address the biggest hurdles here. You might also explore targeted bar prep programs proven to help students with similar starting points succeed. Many applicants in your shoes have improved their profiles and gained admission elsewhere—persistence and focused improvements can change the story for your next application.
